text: This video presents the surgical technique for an endoscopic assisted extra-elevator abdoperineal excision or ELAP. An eighty-seven years old male patient presented a distal rectal cancer located at the level of the anorectal junction. He had an ASA score of three and a BMI of twenty-nine. The CT scan did not show any evidence of distant metastasis. In addition, the MRI of the pelvis showed a tumor, stage MT-NN, located at the level of the anorectal junction. Of note, the CRM was not treated. Finally, the patient underwent a preoperative radiochemotherapy with This is the MRI showing with an arrow the tumor location just at the anorectal junction.

The surgeon begins the abdominal phase of the procedure using a laparoscopic setup with a 12mm camera and three 5mm ports. A medial to lateral dissection is performed, with high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) using Hemolock clips. The total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection proceeds anteriorly to the vesicles and posteriorly to the Valderia fascia. The ureters and hypogastric nerves are identified and preserved. The peritoneum is dissected on the medial side, and the left ureter is seen and preserved. The peritoneum on the lateral side of the rectum is opened with a hook, dividing lateral attachments. Anterior dissection reveals the vesicles, while posterior dissection is challenging due to previous radiotherapy. The narrow pelvis and patient's obesity complicate the dissection process.

The surgeon begins the perineal phase by closing the anus and performing an extra-sphincteric dissection. The skin is opened using a diathermy, and a Lone Star retractor is applied for optimal exposure. A running suture is placed on the skin edge before inserting the gel point port, ensuring a secure seal. Pneumopelvis is initiated with a pressure of twelve. Dissection starts posteriorly at the coccyx level, following the TME plane. The pelvic floor is transected laterally, and muscles on the right side are also transected according to ELAP principles.

The surgeon continues to follow the TME plane cranially, joining the previous dissection. The levator muscles on the left side are completely divided using a hook. The anterior plane dissection is resumed with the hook, while managing the pneumopelvis stability and smoke. Lateral dissection is performed to avoid deep working areas. Anterior dissection proceeds with caution around the prostate to prevent bleeding and nerve injury. Posteriorly, the junction with the abdominal team is achieved, who assist by retracting the specimen. The anterior plane is reopened, and communication with the abdominal team is established. The last lateral attachments are divided using a ligature. The specimen is freed and extracted through the anus with an Alexi wound protector. Hemostasis is checked, a full washout is performed, and a routine drain is placed in the pelvis through a five-millimeter port. An end colostomy is formed, and the perineal wound is closed in layers.
